expectations.text: Response recommends rebasing on origin/main BEFORE pushing
expectations.evidence: Step 2 explicitly instructs 'git fetch origin' followed by 'git rebase origin/main' before Step 3 (Push the branch). The summary at the bottom also shows '# 1. Sync: git fetch origin && git rebase origin/main' listed before '# 2. Push'.

expectations.text: Response mentions `--force-with-lease` for pushing after a rebase
expectations.evidence: Lines 43 and 48 contain the literal text '--force-with-lease'. The response says 'If you rebased after a previous push, use --force-with-lease instead' and provides the command 'git push --force-with-lease'.

expectations.text: Response includes post-merge cleanup: worktree remove + branch delete + prune
expectations.evidence: The 'After the PR merges' section includes all three steps: 'git worktree remove ../myrepo-wt/feat/payments', 'git branch -d feat/payments', and 'git worktree prune'. These are also echoed in the summary section.

expectations.text: Response uses conventional commits format for the PR title (type(scope): subject)
expectations.evidence: The gh pr create command uses '--title "feat(payments): <short description of what you built>"', which follows the conventional commits format type(scope): subject. The summary also shows 'gh pr create --title "feat(payments): ..."'.

suggestions: The response is well-structured and covers all required elements. One minor improvement: the force-with-lease guidance could be more prominently placed — it is currently conditional ('if you rebased after a previous push'), but since Step 2 always does a rebase, the force-with-lease push could be presented as the default push command when a prior push exists.
eval_feedback.overall: Excellent response. All four assertions pass. The response correctly sequences rebase before push, mentions --force-with-lease with proper context, includes the full post-merge cleanup sequence (worktree remove, branch delete, prune), and demonstrates conventional commit format in the PR title example. The response is well-organized and worktree-aware.
